quick_facts.label: Core purpose
quick_facts.value: Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) Officers establish or support results frameworks, define and track indicators, assess programme progress and outcomes, analyse data, and prepare evidence-based reports for managers, government counterparts, donors, and partners.

quick_facts.label: Relevant education routes
quick_facts.value: For Indian public-health and development programmes, published employer requirements have included master's-level study in public health, health management, community/social development, development economics, management, and other social sciences. This makes the node’s MPH and MSW parent qualifications relevant routes, though each vacancy sets its own eligibility criteria.

quick_facts.label: Experience expectation
quick_facts.value: Requirements vary sharply by seniority: a WHO India National Professional Officer (M&E) notice required two years of relevant public-health-programme experience, while an earlier India-based UNDP RBSK M&E Officer vacancy required at least 10 years of relevant post-master's experience.

quick_facts.label: Indian employment contexts
quick_facts.value: The role appears in government-linked health systems and development programmes: examples include WHO India support to national health programmes, the UNDP-supported RBSK unit in New Delhi, and National Health Mission recruitment/appointment activity for District Monitoring and Evaluation Officers in Madhya Pradesh.

sections.heading: What a Monitoring and Evaluation Officer does
paragraphs: In public-health interventions, the role turns programme plans into measurable evidence. Typical work includes helping design monitoring and evaluation plans; developing logframes, indicator matrices, data-collection formats and supportive-supervision tools; setting baselines and targets; and checking whether implementation is progressing against agreed indicators.

The officer also validates and analyses incoming programme data, synthesises results in tables, graphs, presentations and periodic reports, and identifies achievements, gaps, bottlenecks, lessons and corrective actions. The work commonly involves coordinating with implementing teams, government authorities, technical partners and information-system specialists so that reporting processes and data standards are consistent.

Evaluation duties can extend beyond routine reporting: WHO's published role description includes support for mid-term evaluation methodology and consolidation of evaluation reports, while the UNDP India RBSK role included operationalising an integrated M&E system and supplying analysed information to the programme unit and the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare.
bullets: Design or maintain results frameworks, logframes, indicator definitions, baselines, targets and reporting matrices.
Develop or improve data-collection, monitoring and supportive-supervision tools.
Clean, validate, analyse and interpret programme data; communicate findings through reports, dashboards, tables, graphs and presentations.
Support periodic reviews and evaluations, document lessons learned, and recommend action when delivery is off track.
Coordinate reporting and data use with programme teams, government counterparts, implementing partners and donors.

sections.heading: Entry routes from MPH and MSW
paragraphs: An MPH is a direct route where the position is embedded in health programmes, because employers may specifically seek public-health education and experience with health-programme monitoring. WHO India's published M&E role listed a university degree in medicine or public health as essential and postgraduate study in preventive/social medicine, community medicine, public health or business administration as desirable.

An MSW can be a relevant route particularly for community, social-development and implementation-facing programmes. An India-based UNDP M&E Officer vacancy accepted master's qualifications in community/social development and other social sciences alongside public health and health management. Candidates from either route become more competitive by gaining supervised experience in field implementation, routine data systems, surveys, research, reporting or programme coordination.

There is no single statutory Indian licence or one nationally uniform qualification for this job title evident from these employer sources. Applicants should therefore read each recruitment notice for its subject, experience, language, software and location requirements.
bullets: MPH route: build exposure to epidemiology, biostatistics, health information systems, programme management, evaluation methods and public-health field placements.
MSW route: build exposure to community development, research methods, participatory monitoring, implementation management, safeguarding and outcome reporting.
For either route: seek internships or entry-level work involving data quality checks, surveys, dashboard/report preparation, HMIS/MIS work, programme reviews or partner coordination.
Document demonstrable outputs such as indicator trackers, analysis plans, data-collection tools, evaluation protocols, reports or presentations.

sections.heading: Skills employers look for
paragraphs: The role combines quantitative and programme-management capability. Published India-focused role descriptions call for analysis of programme data, development of monitoring frameworks and indicators, use of Excel or statistical software, data collection, report writing, and presentation of findings. The WHO India notice also emphasised rigorous analysis, clear and influential presentation, computer skills, coordination and collaboration.

Practical M&E competency includes understanding results-based management: distinguishing activities, outputs and outcomes; defining usable indicators; specifying data sources and collection frequency; checking data quality; and using findings to improve implementation. Communication matters because officers must make technical evidence intelligible to programme managers, government counterparts, field teams and partners.

For public-health and social programmes, professional practice also requires sensitivity to equity, inclusion, confidentiality and ethical data handling. These requirements are especially important when data concern communities, children, patients or other vulnerable populations.
bullets: Indicator, logframe and results-framework design.
Quantitative and qualitative data collection, cleaning, validation, analysis and interpretation.
Excel and, where required, statistical, database, GIS, HMIS or other information-system tools.
Survey and evaluation protocol/tool development; baseline, midline and endline work.
Clear technical writing, report production, data visualisation and presentation.
Stakeholder coordination, training or capacity building, and practical problem solving.
Ethical, confidential and inclusive handling of programme data.

sections.heading: Where the work is done
paragraphs: In India, M&E Officers can work within public-health programme units, state or district health systems, government-supported missions, UN and multilateral technical-assistance offices, NGOs, research or implementation partners, and donor-funded projects. Work is often a mix of office-based analysis and reporting with coordination across national, state, district and community levels.

The work can involve liaising with government and non-government institutions, field offices and partner organisations; reviewing data received from multiple implementation sites; and supporting monitoring visits, training or review meetings. The exact balance between desk analysis and travel depends on the programme, geography and contract.
bullets: National, state, district or facility-linked public-health programmes.
Government missions and health-system implementation units.
UN, multilateral and donor-supported technical-assistance projects.
Non-governmental organisations and research/implementation partners delivering health or social interventions.

sections.heading: Career progression
paragraphs: Career development is employer- and programme-dependent rather than governed by one universal Indian ladder. An early-career practitioner may begin with data collection, reporting, research assistance or district-level monitoring responsibilities, then move toward independent framework design, evaluation management, technical assistance, capacity building and coordination across multiple geographies or partners.

Senior posts may require substantial prior M&E-system experience. For example, the historical UNDP RBSK India vacancy sought experience establishing M&E frameworks and managing health-project M&E systems at state or national level, while WHO India described work supporting comprehensive M&E planning, evaluation methodology and country-office performance assessment. Possible onward titles therefore include M&E Specialist, MERL Officer/Manager, Programme Manager, Research or Health-Information-System Specialist, or technical adviser, but titles and thresholds vary by organisation.
bullets: Build from routine data and reporting tasks toward framework design, evaluation planning and data-use leadership.
Gain experience across field implementation, HMIS/MIS, surveys, evaluation and multi-stakeholder coordination.
Develop a portfolio showing tools, analyses, reports and evidence of improvements made from findings.
Treat job titles as variable: assess the actual scope, grade, contract type and experience requirements in each vacancy.

sections.heading: Compensation context
paragraphs: Pay is highly variable by employer, grade, location, funding source, contract type and seniority; historical vacancy figures should not be treated as a current market salary benchmark. For dated context, WHO India's 30 June 2023 National Professional Officer (M&E) notice stated an annual base salary starting at INR 2,626,256, subject to applicable mandatory deductions. A much earlier UNDP India RBSK posting, with a 10-year post-master's experience requirement, listed INR 127,175 per month taxable.

Before comparing offers, check whether the stated amount is monthly or annual; gross or net; employee, consultant or service-contract compensation; and whether pension, insurance, travel or other benefits are included. Verify the live recruitment notice because historical figures, grades and contract conditions may no longer apply.
bullets: Use current vacancy notices, not old listings, for compensation decisions.
Compare role scope and experience threshold alongside salary; both cited examples were senior or specialised posts.
Check deductions, benefits, contract duration and whether remuneration is taxable before evaluating an offer.
